blocks.text: Barbara Ward
Director of Operations
Garza, Leblanc and Porter
Re: Engagement to develop a pricing strategy
Dear Ms. Ward:

This letter confirms that we will work with Garza, Leblanc and Porter on its pricing, and it sets down the terms of that work: what we will do, who will do it, the fee, and the conditions we hold ourselves to. I have kept it plain. If a term here does not match our discussions, telephone me before you sign, and we will correct it.
What We Will Do

The subject is why the prices you set on paper are not the prices you collect, and what to do about it. The work does four things. First, we measure your price realization: the true gap between list and collected price once discounts, rebates, freight, and terms are counted. Second, we map margin across your products and your accounts, so the places you earn and the places you give money back are visible instead of assumed. Third, we document how prices are set today, who may discount, and how far that authority runs before anyone reviews it. Fourth, we hand you a pricing approach you can operate: the structure of the list, the rules on discounting, and the order in which to make the changes.

Four things sit outside this work, and I will name them so our expectations match from the start. We do not audit your books or opine on your statements. We do not give legal or tax advice; where a question turns on either, we flag it and send you to your counsel. We do not approach your customers or renegotiate their contracts. And we do not choose or install software; where the work reaches a systems question, we frame it and stop.
How We Work

We work from your own numbers, not from rules of thumb, and every figure in a deliverable ties back to a workpaper we can produce on request. We keep findings and recommendations apart, and label each, because they do not carry the same weight and should not be read as though they did. And we bring you interim results as they harden, so that nothing in the final material arrives as a surprise to you or your people.
Who Will Do the Work

Brenda Rodriguez, Senior Consultant, runs the engagement day to day and is your contact on anything of substance. She builds the realization and margin analysis the recommendations stand on. Deborah Gordon, Associate, turns that analysis into the findings and the written material you receive. Mary Parker, Research Associate, assembles the underlying data and keeps the record of what we were given and by whom. I read everything before it leaves this office. On your side, we will treat you, Barbara Ward, as our point of contact for scheduling, data, and any decision the work requires, unless you name someone else.
What We Ask of You

The work depends on timely access to your pricing, invoice, and rebate records, and to the people who set prices day to day. We will send one consolidated data request at the start and keep a log of what is outstanding, and we ask that requests to your staff route through your office so that nobody is asked twice for the same file. Some of what we need touches commercial terms with named customers; tell us where a restriction applies and we will observe it.
Fee and Start

Our fee to {{fact:f:E-2009-004.client}} is {{fact:f:E-2009-004.fee}}, fixed for the scope above. We begin on {{fact:f:E-2009-004.start}} and expect about seven months from that point to the final recommendations. If you ask us to widen the scope, we price the addition in writing and do not start it until you have agreed the price. We prefer a fixed fee here so that your people can work with us without watching a clock.
Billing

We invoice the fixed fee in equal monthly installments across the term, each due within thirty days of its date. Out-of-pocket costs, chiefly travel to your sites, are billed at cost and itemized on the invoice. We do not mark them up.
Term and Ending the Engagement

The engagement runs from the start date above until we deliver the final recommendations, unless one of us ends it sooner. Either party may terminate on fifteen days' written notice, for any reason or none. On termination you owe the fee for work done to the effective date, prorated against the fixed fee, together with expenses incurred, and we hand over our work in whatever state it has then reached.
Handling Your Information

Pricing is among the most sensitive information a company holds, and we treat it that way. We will use what you give us only for this engagement and disclose it outside our firm only as you direct in writing or as the law requires. We ask the same of you regarding our methods, our workpapers, and the fee arrangement recorded in this letter.
Limits on Our Liability

Our total liability arising out of this engagement, whether framed in contract, in tort, or on any other basis, is limited to the fees actually paid to us under it. Neither party will be liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss, including lost profits, however it arises. This limit does not reach our own fraud or willful misconduct. Our deliverables are prepared for your internal use; if you intend to give one to a third party who may rely on it, raise it with me first so we can address the reliance in writing.
Governing Law and Disputes

This letter is governed by the law of the state in which our principal office sits, without regard to its conflict of laws rules. Any dispute we cannot settle between us will go to mediation before either party begins litigation.

If these terms suit you, please sign both copies of this letter and return one to me; the other is yours to keep. We are glad to take on this work for Garza, Leblanc and Porter, and Brenda Rodriguez will be ready to begin on the start date set out above.
Sincerely,
To: Deborah Gordon, Brenda Rodriguez
From: Mary Parker
Subject: Kickoff, pricing strategy for Garza, Leblanc and Porter

We begin the pricing work for Garza, Leblanc and Porter on {{fact:f:E-2009-004.start}}, and I want the three of us starting from the same map. What {{fact:f:E-2009-004.client}} has asked is simple to say and harder to answer well: they want to know why the prices they set are not the prices they collect, and they want a way to set and hold prices that they can actually run. Everything below serves that one question. Barbara Ward, their Director of Operations, is our point of contact, and she has offered her operations and finance people for the data we will need.
The shape of the work

items: Price realization comes first. Brenda Rodriguez will build the diagnostic that measures the gap between list and collected price, once discounts, rebates, freight, and terms are counted. I will gather the source data and record where each file came from, so that the first time the client questions a number we can show our working rather than rebuild it.

Margin by product and account grows out of the same data. This is where we see where the company earns and where it quietly gives money back. Brenda Rodriguez owns the analysis; Deborah Gordon and I will keep the product and account definitions consistent, because a margin figure is worthless if two of us are counting it differently.

How prices get set today is as much interview as arithmetic. We want to understand who quotes, who may discount, and how far that authority runs before anyone reviews it. Deborah Gordon will lead those conversations on the client side and draw the pattern out of them.

The pricing approach is last, and it rests on the first three. We will not sketch a recommendation before the diagnostic supports it. When we reach it, Deborah Gordon drafts and Brenda Rodriguez tests the numbers underneath.

blocks.text: A word on working with the client. Barbara Ward has asked for a light touch on her people's time, which is fair. We honor it by asking once, asking clearly, and keeping our own record straight so that we are not back for the same file twice. Where the data touches commercial terms with named customers, we handle it as confidential and keep it inside the team.
The first two weeks

items: I will send a single consolidated data request this week and open a log of what we have asked for and what has come back, so that nobody on Barbara Ward's staff is asked twice for the same file.
Brenda Rodriguez will fix the definitions for price, discount, and margin before any number is calculated, so that we are not renegotiating what a figure means in the third month.
Deborah Gordon will schedule the first interviews on how prices are set and draft the questions for us to review together.
The three of us sit down at the end of next week to walk through what the data is telling us so far, gaps included.

blocks.text: At this stage the client sees nothing that reads as a finding, because we have none yet. We have a scope, a plan, and a data request going out. I will keep the log current so that we always know where each request stands. If anything in this plan looks wrong to either of you, say so now, while it costs nothing to change.

To: Barbara Ward
Cc: Deborah Gordon, Brenda Rodriguez
Barbara,

Thank you for getting the first tranche of pricing and invoice data to us so quickly. Brenda Rodriguez has started building the realization picture for {{fact:f:E-2009-004.client}} from it, and the early cut already shows the kind of spread between list and collected price that made this work worth doing. We are not drawing conclusions yet; there is more to fold in before the numbers hold still.

Two items would help us keep pace. We are still short the rebate and allowance detail for the last two years, which we need before the realization figure means anything, and we would like the discount-authority guidelines your sales managers work to, if such a document exists. Everything we have received from the Garza, Leblanc and Porter side so far has come in clean and well labeled, which makes the work faster.

Deborah Gordon is lining up the interviews on how prices get set. Could you point us to two or three people who quote and discount day to day? A half hour with each over the next couple of weeks is all we need to start.
Thanks again,
Mary Parker

To: Barbara Ward
Cc: Deborah Gordon, Brenda Rodriguez
Barbara,

That was quick, thank you. The three names you sent are exactly the right people, and Deborah Gordon will reach out to each of them directly to find a half hour. If it is easier on your side, we are glad to come to you and see all three in one afternoon.

The rebate file came through as well, and Brenda Rodriguez has already begun reconciling it against the invoice data. One thing we noticed on a first pass: a handful of the largest accounts carry allowances that do not appear on the standard price sheets, so we will want to understand how those were agreed. Nothing to act on yet; I flag it only so that it is on the record for {{fact:f:E-2009-004.client}} as we go.

We now have most of what we need to give the realization picture a proper first look. Once Brenda Rodriguez has the rebate reconciliation settled and Deborah Gordon has had the first of those conversations, we will pull together a short read for you. I am keeping the request log current, and everything outstanding on the Garza, Leblanc and Porter side comes down to the interviews now.
Thanks again,
Mary Parker
Pricing Strategy: Garza, Leblanc and Porter

items: Client of record: {{fact:f:E-2009-004.client}}
Engagement commenced {{fact:f:E-2009-004.start}}
Prepared for Barbara Ward, Director of Operations
Prepared by Mary Parker, with Deborah Gordon and Brenda Rodriguez
An early-phase briefing, not a set of conclusions

blocks.text: The question we are here to answer

items: Why the prices you set on paper are not the prices you collect
Where your margin is strong and where it quietly erodes
How prices are set today, and who along the way may discount
What a pricing approach you can actually run would look like

blocks.text: How we are working

items: From your own invoice and rebate data, not from rules of thumb
Every figure traceable to a workpaper we can produce on request
Findings and recommendations kept separate and clearly labeled
Interim reads shared with you as they firm up, not held to the end

blocks.text: What we can see so far

Two things stand out even at this early stage, and both need more work before we would put a number on either. The gap between what you list and what you actually collect is real and worth measuring precisely. And the discounting is not spread evenly: it concentrates in the largest accounts, several of which carry allowances that never reach the standard price sheets. We offer these as directions to look, not as conclusions.
What we are still working through

items: Reconciling two years of rebates and allowances against the invoice records
Interviews on who quotes and who may discount, now getting under way
How the off-sheet allowances on the major accounts were first agreed

blocks.text: Where we go from here

items: Close out the realization and margin diagnostic
Finish the pricing-authority interviews with your sales managers
Bring a first set of findings, labeled as findings, to the next session
Keep routing data requests through Barbara Ward's office

blocks.text: Working session on the pricing strategy for Garza, Leblanc and Porter, held at the client's offices. Minutes taken by Brenda Rodriguez.
Present

items: Brenda Rodriguez, Senior Consultant
Mary Parker, Research Associate
Deborah Gordon, Associate
Barbara Ward, Director of Operations, {{fact:f:E-2009-004.client}}

blocks.text: Matters discussed

The session reviewed the price realization diagnostic and the first read on margin. We are far enough in to show a measured realization figure and a margin picture by account and product line. These are findings, and I labeled them as such at the start, but two of them still carry qualifications, which I set out below.

Before the analysis, Mary Parker summarized the state of the data. The invoice, rebate, and allowance files are now in and reconciled, with one exception: the freight terms for a block of smaller accounts arrived in a format we are still normalizing. She was careful to separate what is confirmed from what is still being cleaned, and none of the findings below depend on the block that remains outstanding.

The realization work is complete for the two years of data we were given. The gap between list price and collected price is wider than the client had assumed, and most of it is not the visible line discount. It is the accumulation of rebates, allowances, freight terms, and off-invoice adjustments that no single person sees in one place. I would put weight on this figure. It reconciles to the invoice data, and Mary Parker holds the source for every component.

Deborah Gordon presented the margin view. Margin varies more by account than by product, which points the remedy toward how the company prices customers rather than how it prices goods. One qualification holds. A set of large-account allowances sits off the standard price sheets, and until we confirm how each was authorized, the margin on those accounts is stated within a range rather than as a single number. Barbara Ward agreed to have her commercial staff walk us through the origin of the three largest.

Deborah Gordon also reported on the pricing-authority interviews. She has finished four of the planned conversations and can already describe how a quote moves from request to commitment. Two points are visible: discount authority runs further down the sales organization than the client's own guidelines suggest, and there is no single point at which a discount above a threshold is reviewed before it is granted. She will complete the remaining interviews before committing that pattern to writing.

I want one assumption on the record before it hardens. The realization figure rests on the last two years being representative. Barbara Ward noted that one of those years included a stretch of unusual discounting to hold volume. We will test whether that stretch distorts the picture before any recommendation leans on the two-year average. Mary Parker will isolate the period so we can see the figure with and without it.

We did not discuss recommendations today, and I said at the outset that we would not. The client asked for an early view of how much of the realization gap is recoverable. I declined to give a number. The gap is measured; how much of it can be closed depends on the pricing-authority work and the allowance review, and neither is finished. A figure offered now would be a guess, and it would anchor the client to something we might have to walk back.
Action items

rows: Provide the origin and authorization of the three largest off-sheet allowances
Isolate the unusual-discounting period and restate realization with and without it
Complete the remaining pricing-authority interviews with the sales managers
Reconcile the allowance detail against the affected account margins
Set the date of the next working session once the items above are in hand
